You need to walk back down the walk and feel great about that and eager to get to the next door, right? And so they need a story for that purpose to build up their self-confidence and courage and or to shame or guilt them into desired action. So now, your list may be different by the way, but this is now a way to catalog and categorize your stories as by purpose. So now when you're ready to need one of these, there's a pile of them over here. However, it is you organize things. And I will sometimes like, so not just personal experience, but oh, here's a magazine article, you know about somebody and their life experience and it fits one of these categories, that's where the magazine article goes. So later, I may, because it's not just personal story, it's telling you a story about Arnold Schwarzenegger or a story about Branson or a story about Trump or whatever. I sometimes rip up books and pages from the books go in the different files. So now when I don't have to go dredge through the entire Trump autobiography, because I remember somewhere in there is a story about this. That story's already been another nice way to do it. Of course, it would be to photocopy, but I'd tear the pages out. But now I got it where I needed without having to hunt for it. I confess I'm not as timely and organized about that as I would like, but I'm more organized probably than most people. I mean, I got like five or six drawers of this, so it's not necessarily current, but it's there to be drawn from. And so when you start to think about speed, think about how much time you spend hunting for or trying to recall or trying to think up the example that makes the point you want to make. And if you can cuddle that time out and have the bank there in advance that you draw from and from the standpoint of consistency with your own stories, so you don't want to tell it one way here and another way over here and get a lot of the details wrong, which if you use the same story a lot is quite possible to do. So you want it somewhere where you can pull it and refer to it so that you're not like born in Cleveland in one version of the story and born in Cincinnati in the other version of the story. Or yeah, well, yeah, close counts, but you know, or you know, it was $72,000 in this story and $36,000 in that story, and that stuff will naturally happen if you just rely on memory. So you buy speed by this kind of organization. If you skip to page 83, as I said, your list may and probably should be different than mine, and you should think specifically about what kinds of stories you need, what points you need to make in all of the different ways that you seek to influence your audience. And build your list of purposes. Then you work backwards to find or create or remember or polish the stories to fit the purposes. So let's very quickly take about 15 minutes and help each other at the rounds. So you're going to be testing your businesses and identifying purposes for stories you should have. This becomes, and you should work some more on this obviously when you leave here, but this becomes a checklist now for you to go home and build your files. Here's the points we will need to make. So like we were just talking about your boot camp on the break. So if you're going to sell a boot camp, there's a whole bunch of purposes of stories. That are useful in selling boot camps. So one example, okay, are the people who, one part of everybody's market are the people who bought some of their stuff. Two years ago, three years ago, four years ago, a decade ago, and have never been to a boot camp. Well, we need a story for why somebody who's never taken a shrink wrap off the notebook and never done anything and never gotten any results should still now pony up another 2000 bucks and show up in a room. Right. So that's one purpose you would have for a story. Now you got to think, do I have one in a testimonial form or is there somebody I can get one from or do I need to make one up or is there one about me or is there one about my brother, you know, who had 22, how to hit the baseball programs and hadn't used any of them and finally went to an event with a coach and now he's playing for the Orioles. I mean, so purpose story, purpose story. So take 15 minutes and bunch up in groups back there and work on your purpose list. What do we need stories for and help each other a little bit thinking about your business, each other thinking about each other's business with fresh eyes. What could you use a story for?
